title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who describe their most vivid childhood memories and how those memories affect them today. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Once Upon a Time: Four People on Childhood Memories and Nostalgia

texts.A:
### Diane, 52, Interior Designer
[43]My clearest childhood memory is of my grandmother's kitchen – the smell of bread baking, the sound of the radio, and the warmth that seemed to exist nowhere else in the house.[/43] I spent many afternoons there helping her roll dough without ever producing anything edible. It was only years later that I understood those hours weren't really about cooking at all, but about being taught, wordlessly, what it means to make a house feel like a home. [47]I believe my career as an interior designer was shaped entirely by those afternoons; I am always chasing that same quality of warmth in every space I work on.[/47] [51]My grandmother died when I was nine, and the grief I felt then still surfaces unexpectedly when I smell baked bread in someone else's kitchen.[/51]
texts.B:
### Sam, 39, Electrician
[44]I grew up in a large, noisy family where privacy was almost non-existent, and my escape was a small patch of woodland at the edge of our estate.[/44] My brothers weren't interested in it, so it became entirely mine. I built dens, tracked animals, and spent whole summer days there without any particular plan. [48]I sometimes wonder whether those solitary hours in the woods taught me how to be comfortable with my own company, which I think is a genuinely rare skill.[/48] These days I live in a city flat and the closest I get to that woodland is the local park, but even a few minutes of green space can restore something in me. My eldest son is showing the same instinct, and I try to give him the same freedom I had.
texts.C:
### Nadia, 44, University Professor
[45]I remember sitting on the floor of my father's study surrounded by books I was far too young to understand, feeling a sense of belonging that has never entirely left me.[/45] My father would read aloud to me from whatever he happened to be reading – history, science, novels – without simplifying anything. I didn't always follow the words, but the rhythm and the attention stayed with me. [49]The academic world I now inhabit feels like a natural continuation of those afternoons on my father's study floor, even when it frustrates me.[/49] [52]I've often thought that reading aloud to children, regardless of the subject matter, may be one of the most powerful gifts a parent can offer.[/52]
texts.D:
### Jack, 30, Musician
[46]A memory that has stayed with me more than any other is of watching my uncle play guitar at a family gathering when I was about six years old.[/46] He was not a professional musician – he worked in insurance – but when he played, the whole room changed. Everyone went quiet, and I remember feeling something I couldn't name at the time but now recognise as awe. [50]I picked up the guitar at eight and have never really put it down, so in a very direct sense my entire adult life has grown from that single moment of watching my uncle play.[/50] I've never told him that, but I probably should.

questions.question: has a memory centred on a specific sensory experience in a family member's home?
questions.explanation: Diane's childhood memory is of her grandmother's kitchen – the smell of baking bread, the sound of the radio, and the warmth there.

questions.question: found an outdoor space that became a private refuge from a busy family environment?
questions.explanation: Sam grew up in a large noisy family and escaped to a patch of woodland at the edge of their estate that became entirely his.

questions.question: describes feeling a sense of belonging in an environment associated with intellectual activity?
questions.explanation: Nadia describes sitting on the floor of her father's study surrounded by books and feeling a sense of belonging that has never left her.

questions.question: has a defining memory connected to watching someone they admired perform?
questions.explanation: Jack's most enduring memory is of watching his uncle play guitar at a family gathering when he was around six.

questions.question: believes a childhood experience directly determined the career they went on to pursue?
questions.explanation: Diane believes her career as an interior designer was shaped entirely by the afternoons in her grandmother's kitchen.

questions.question: suggests that a childhood habit helped them develop a particular personal quality?
questions.explanation: Sam wonders whether the solitary hours in the woods taught him to be comfortable with his own company, which he sees as a rare skill.

questions.question: sees a clear connection between their early experiences and the world they now work in?
questions.explanation: Nadia says the academic world she inhabits feels like a natural continuation of those afternoons in her father's study.

questions.question: credits a single childhood moment with determining the entire direction of their adult life?
questions.explanation: Jack says his entire adult life has grown from the single moment of watching his uncle play guitar, as he started playing at eight and never stopped.

questions.question: mentions that a childhood memory is linked to a grief that still affects them today?
questions.explanation: Diane says her grandmother died when she was nine and the grief still surfaces unexpectedly when she smells baked bread.

questions.question: draws a broader conclusion about parenting based on their own childhood experience?
questions.explanation: Nadia concludes that reading aloud to children, regardless of subject matter, may be one of the most powerful gifts a parent can offer.
